artifacts.content:
From: Daniel Whitmore <daniel.whitmore@gmail.com>
To: Sarah Jenkins <sjenkins@volterrafietta.com>
Date: 2026-03-04 09:18
Subject: Follow-up information – Associate (Investment Arbitration/Public International Law)

Dear Sarah,

Many thanks again for the conversation yesterday and for outlining the likely structure of the Volterra Fietta recruitment process. As discussed, I’m sending through a few clarifications so you have everything to brief the partners ahead of a potential second-round interview.

On current remuneration, my package at Herbert Smith Freehills is £122,000 per annum base salary, plus discretionary bonus (most recent bonus: £12,000). Benefits are fairly standard City firm: pension, private medical, and a small firm-wide profit share.

In terms of notice period and availability, my contract requires three months’ notice. The timing is slightly complicated by a family commitment: Emma is scheduled for an elective caesarean at Homerton on 20 June 2026, and I’ve agreed with my current team that I’ll take two consecutive weeks of shared parental leave immediately afterwards to help with the newborn and with Oliver. Realistically, that means I’d only be in a position to resign in early July 2026, once we know there are no complications. That would make a likely start date with you around early October 2026, depending on how HSF treats the parental leave within my three‑month notice period.

I should also be honest that I’d ideally like to take a few weeks off between roles to decompress after what is shaping up to be a very intense year of filings and hearings.

Please let me know if you’d like anything in writing on my current caseload or references.

Best regards,

Daniel

Daniel Edward Whitmore
Associate, International Arbitration
Herbert Smith Freehills LLP

metadata.subject: Follow-up information – Associate (Investment Arbitration/Public International Law)

artifacts.content:
From: HR London Recruitment <recruitment.london@hsf.com>
To: Daniel Whitmore <daniel.whitmore@hsf.com>
Date: 2023-06-02 09:14
Subject: Formal Offer – Associate (International Arbitration)

Dear Daniel,

Further to our recent discussions, we are pleased to confirm our formal offer of employment for the role of Associate (International Arbitration) at Herbert Smith Freehills LLP, London.

Salary and benefits
• Starting base salary: £110,000 per annum, payable monthly in arrears.
• Discretionary performance-related bonus: currently targeted at up to 10% of base salary, subject to firm and individual performance.
• Pension: 5% employer contribution, subject to scheme rules.
• Private medical insurance and life assurance in line with firm policy.

Please note that the above salary reflects your starting point on the associate scale as at September 2023 and will be subject to annual review in line with firm-wide salary review processes. Any future increases, bonus awards or adjustments to benefits will be communicated separately in writing.

This offer is conditional upon satisfactory references, right-to-work checks, and confirmation of your practising certificate status with the SRA. For the avoidance of doubt, this letter does not constitute tax or financial advice; you should seek independent advice regarding PAYE, pension contributions and student loan repayments.

Kindly confirm your acceptance of this offer by signing and returning the attached contract by 16 June 2023. If you have any questions about the salary, bonus structure or benefits package, please do not hesitate to contact me or the Graduate Recruitment team.

We look forward to welcoming you to Herbert Smith Freehills.

Kind regards,

Sarah Jenkins
HR Business Partner – Disputes
Herbert Smith Freehills LLP

metadata.subject: Formal Offer – Associate (International Arbitration)

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Turner <michael.turner@legal-talent.co.uk>
To: Daniel Whitmore <daniel.whitmore@gmail.com>
Date: 2025-11-18 18:47
Subject: Updated Salary Benchmarking – Int’l Arbitration Associates

Hi Daniel,

As discussed, I’m sending over some updated salary benchmarking for London-based international arbitration associates with 5–7 PQE.

For candidates at the larger City firms, we’re generally seeing:

• Band 1 / Magic Circle: £145,000–£155,000 base (typical mid-point around £150,000), plus discretionary bonus of 15–25% depending on hours and firm performance.
• Band 2 / Large International Firms: £130,000–£140,000 base, with bonuses typically in the 10–20% range.
• Specialist arbitration boutiques: more variability, but often £125,000–£135,000 base with a higher upside on bonus or profit-share for strong billers.

From our last conversation, you mentioned that your current package is “a bit below the market mid-point” and that you’re keen not to move for less than a total compensation opportunity in the £150k–£160k range (including bonus). That’s entirely realistic given your ICSID and public international law experience.

If we progress discussions with the boutique I mentioned, they indicated they could likely come in around £135,000 base with a target bonus of 20%, and potentially a signing bonus to offset any lost year-end payment at your current firm.

Let’s pick this up on Friday after you’ve spoken to Emma about childcare logistics and the mortgage planning. I know you’re rightly wary of taking on higher fixed costs after the back issues you mentioned and the possibility of needing physio.

Best,

Michael

metadata.subject: Updated Salary Benchmarking – Int’l Arbitration Associates

artifacts.content:
Title: Annual Compensation Review – Int’l Arbitration Associates

Notes:
Agenda:
1. Overview of 2025–26 financial performance
2. Associate salary bands and market comparison
   - Proposed IA associate band for 5–6 PQE: £118,000–£128,000 base
   - Target discretionary bonus range: 8–18% of base
3. Individual performance discussion (Daniel)
   - Billable hours: slightly above team average
   - Positive feedback from partner on ICSID filing
   - Development areas: delegation and matter management
4. Provisional recommendation (subject to partner approval):
   - Move Daniel to mid-point of proposed band (indicative figure discussed: £125,000 base)
   - Bonus to be aligned with team outcome; no individual adjustment proposed
5. Non-financial points
   - Hybrid working pattern and WFH equipment (chair/standing desk given back pain)
   - Longer-term progression: path to Senior Associate, potential secondment

Internal note (HR only):
Daniel has expressed concern about London living costs and nursery fees and indicated he would consider external opportunities if he feels he is materially behind market. We should be prepared to explain the firm’s broader benefits and promotion prospects.

Please bring the printed salary banding sheet and last year’s bonus pool breakdown for reference.

metadata.title: Annual Compensation Review – Int’l Arbitration Associates

artifacts.content:
From: Emma Whitmore <emma.whitmore@gmail.com>
To: Daniel Whitmore <daniel.whitmore@gmail.com>
Date: 2025-09-05 21:03
Subject: Budget spreadsheet + nursery fees

Dan,

I’ve updated the budget spreadsheet after the new nursery fees came through and thought it might help to see it in black and white.

Key points:
• Oliver’s nursery from October: £1,420/month.
• Our rent: £2,050/month.
• Combined take-home (after tax, NI, pension, student loan): just under £7,000/month based on your current after-tax income of around £4,700 and mine of about £2,250.

I know you said HR mentioned the associate salary scale going up this year and that you “should be somewhere in the low one-hundreds” gross now, but until we see it actually hit your payslip I’m reluctant to bank on it. Even if your gross is, say, £115,000 with a small bonus at the end of the year, it doesn’t change the monthly picture massively.

I’ve put a tab in the spreadsheet showing what things would look like if your gross went up to £135,000 and we overpaid the mortgage by £300/month once we eventually move. At the moment, though, we’re pretty tight after childcare and travel, especially when you have to pay for physio.

Not trying to stress you out, just want us to be realistic before we commit to a bigger place. Maybe that Portugal trip with Soph and Tom needs to be more low-key than we’d hoped.

Love,

E x

metadata.subject: Budget spreadsheet + nursery fees

artifacts.content:
Title: Call with boutique PIL/Investment Arbitration firm – compensation discussion

Description:
Introductory call with managing partner of boutique PIL/IA firm to discuss potential lateral move. Focus on role scope, team structure, and indicative compensation package.

Talking points Daniel prepared:
• Current situation: City firm associate role with base in the "low six figures" and modest discretionary bonus (last year’s total comp c. £135k including bonus and benefits, by his rough calculation).
• Desired package if moving:
  - Base salary not less than £130,000.
  - Bonus potential up to 25% of base for strong performance.
  - Clarity on any profit-share or long-term incentive arrangements.
• Other considerations:
  - School/nursery fees and rent in Shoreditch – needs predictable monthly income.
  - Back problems mean he values proper WFH set-up and private medical cover.
  - Concerned about volatility of boutique revenue vs. stability of current firm.

Notes to self (in calendar entry):
- Be honest but not overly specific about current base; frame it as “just over £115,000 plus a relatively small bonus” rather than quoting exact figures.
- Ask directly whether they would consider a signing bonus to compensate for any year-end payment lost if moving before December.
- Remember to ask about pay review cycle (annual vs. ad hoc) and whether salary is pegged to PQE band or purely merit-based.

Dial-in details in email from Michael (recruiter). Take call from home after Oliver’s bedtime.

metadata.title: Call with boutique PIL/Investment Arbitration firm – compensation discussion

artifacts.content:
From: Sarah McIntyre <sarah.mcintyre@legalsearchpartners.co.uk>
To: Daniel Whitmore <daniel.whitmore@gmail.com>
Date: 2026-01-19 09:12
Subject: HSF benchmarking & comp expectations

Hi Daniel,

Lovely to speak on Friday and thanks again for being so open about where you are in your career.

Further to our chat, I’ve pulled some benchmarking for 5–6 PQE international arbitration associates at the larger City firms. At your level, we’re typically seeing:

- Base salaries in the £135,000–£145,000 range at US outfits
- Magic Circle and top international firms clustering around £110,000–£118,000 base
- Discretionary bonuses varying quite a bit, but c. 10–15% is a reasonable working assumption for solid performers

You mentioned that your current package at Herbert Smith Freehills is “roughly in line” with the mid‑point of that Magic Circle band, and that last year’s bonus was noticeably lighter than the year before. When we come to formal processes, it will help if you can give me a clean headline number (e.g. "about £115k plus bonus") that you’re comfortable sharing with partners, rather than the more nuanced explanation you gave me on the phone.

On timing, I heard what you said about not wanting to drop your current team in it. Because of a couple of big hearings you’re staffed on in the next quarter, it sounds like it might realistically be closer to four months before you could move, even though your contractual notice is three.

Let’s touch base next week once I’ve spoken to the boutique you’re interested in.

Best,
Sarah

metadata.subject: HSF benchmarking & comp expectations

Subject: Confirmation of 2026 Salary Review – International Arbitration Associates

Dear Daniel,

Following the recent salary review, we are pleased to confirm your revised remuneration with effect from 1 January 2026.

Your new base salary will be £118,500 (one hundred and eighteen thousand five hundred pounds) per annum, payable in accordance with the firm’s usual payroll arrangements and subject to statutory deductions. This represents an increase of 5% on your current base salary.

For the avoidance of doubt, this figure does not include any discretionary bonus which may be awarded under the firm’s associate bonus scheme. Bonuses are determined by reference to individual performance, team performance and overall firm profitability; any such payment is not guaranteed and remains entirely discretionary.

All other terms and conditions of your employment remain unchanged, including your current benefits package (pension, private medical insurance, life assurance and income protection).

Please note that this letter supersedes the indicative figures discussed during your mid‑year review, where a provisional range of £116,000–£120,000 was mentioned. The final amount has now been approved by the Partnership Remuneration Committee.

Laura Jenkins

Dear all,

As part of our annual SRA compliance process, we are required to confirm that all practising solicitors have up-to-date details on file. This includes your SRA roll number, admission date and any practising conditions.

Our records show the following for you, Daniel Whitmore:

• Status: Associate – International Arbitration
• Admitted in England & Wales: 2017
• SRA reference on file: 502761 (to be confirmed by you)

Please check this against your practising certificate. If any of the above is incorrect, reply to this email with the correct information so that we can update the central register before it is provided to the COLP.

If you have ever held a practising certificate under a different name (e.g. before marriage or by deed poll), please include those details. You should also disclose any historic or ongoing SRA investigations, findings or conditions, however minor. Non-disclosure may itself constitute a regulatory breach.

Daniel – you mentioned in last year’s appraisal that you were considering a move to a public international law boutique. If you do commence any formal lateral processes, please ensure that the SRA number you give to recruiters matches what is on your current certificate, not the old figure we used in your trainee records.

Lucy Patel

Herbert Smith Freehills LLP

Subject: Quick clarification on VF application form

Dear Maria,

Sorry to bother you in the evening, but I’m just finishing the online application for the associate role and there are a couple of fields I want to get right.

One asks for my “qualifications date” as a solicitor. I’ve put 2017-09-15, which is the date on my admission letter from the SRA confirming that I was admitted as a solicitor of the Senior Courts of England and Wales. I assume that’s what they’re after, rather than the date I completed the LPC.

There’s also a section on “connections with the firm”. I’ve drafted a short explanation noting that I know you from LSE – that you informally supervised my LLM dissertation after Professor James Crawford had to step back in March 2016 when his cancer recurred, and that we met twice a week between March and June 2016 to rescue the project after I’d failed the first draft in February due to missed deadlines, anxiety and insomnia. I’ve also mentioned that we caught up for coffee near Lincoln’s Inn in October 2023, when I asked you (somewhat nervously) whether my negative mid‑year appraisal at HSF, for missing a couple of internal deadlines on an ICSID case, would make me a non‑starter at VF, and that you encouraged me to apply anyway.

If any of that feels inappropriate to include, please say and I’ll tone it down.

Best,
Daniel

metadata.subject: Quick clarification on VF application form

artifacts.content:

Subject: Confirmation of Admission as a Solicitor – September 2017

Dear Daniel,

Further to your successful completion of the LPC and your training contract, we write to confirm the key dates around your admission as a solicitor of the Senior Courts of England and Wales.

Your admission application has been approved by the SRA and you are scheduled to be entered on the roll during the September 2017 admission round. For our internal HR records, we have provisionally recorded your **qualification date as 01/09/2017**, which will also be used for salary banding and PQE calculations unless the SRA subsequently confirms a different date.

Please check that this provisional qualification date (1 September 2017) aligns with your understanding and let us know by Friday 7 July if there are any discrepancies in your documentation or if the SRA has indicated another date.

Once your qualification date has been finalised, we will issue an updated statement confirming your status as an Associate in the International Arbitration group with effect from early September. Note that your contractual three‑month notice period will run from the date you sign your permanent associate contract, which we anticipate will be shortly after your qualification date.

Sarah McKenzie

Subject: Further information for Associate (Investment Arbitration) application

Dear Sir or Madam,

Further to my online application submitted this evening, I wanted to clarify a couple of points that did not fit neatly into the form fields.

First, on the question about my admission as a solicitor: I completed my training contract at Herbert Smith Freehills in August 2017 and was admitted to the roll shortly thereafter. For the avoidance of doubt, my **qualification date is recorded internally at HSF as 30/08/2017** for PQE purposes. If you require documentary evidence, I can provide my SRA admission letter and practising certificate.

Second, regarding notice period and availability, my contract states a three‑month notice period. However, because I am currently on a formal performance improvement plan at Herbert Smith Freehills LLP following critical feedback from my supervising partner, Jonathan Mills, in October 2025, HR have indicated that they would be prepared to waive part of my contractual three‑month notice period if I resign before my next review meeting on 10 April 2026 at Exchange House in London; in practice this means I could potentially be available to start at Volterra Fietta in as little as four to six weeks, depending on how much of my accrued annual leave (currently seven days) they require me to use up before my last day.

I hope this is helpful, but please let me know if you need any further information.

Kind regards,

Daniel Whitmore

artifacts.content:
From: Richard Whitmore <richard.whitmore@btinternet.com>
To: Daniel Whitmore <daniel.whitmore@hsf.com>
Date: 2020-01-14 20:47
Subject: That Hague case and your qualification date

Hi Dan,

I’ve just had another call about that construction arbitration in The Hague where I did the expert witness work. You’ll remember it’s the one where Volterra Fietta were acting for the Latin American state. They’re asking whether I can confirm the exact dates I was instructed, and it got me thinking about your own dates for CV purposes.

On my consultancy paperwork I’ve put that I started assisting them in **November 2019** and wrapped up around May 2020. When I was filling in the conflicts questionnaire, I listed you as a relative working in international arbitration and noted that you’d been qualified as a solicitor since **September 2016** (which I now realise might be wrong – I think you actually qualified the following year, didn’t you?).

I also have an indirect connection to Volterra Fietta through that same matter: during 2019–2020 I rang you several evenings from my home office in Winchester to ask general questions about ICSID procedure and whether it was normal for counsel to push so hard on revising my draft report. You told me that this was standard and that Volterra Fietta had a reputation for very robust cross‑examination prep. I later felt rather bullied by one particular partner and regretted accepting the instruction, so please don’t mention my name if you ever apply there – I’d hate it to affect my pension‑top‑up consultancy work.

Anyway, can you remind me of the precise month and year you actually qualified so I can correct my notes?

Love,
Dad

From: Graduate Recruitment <trainee.recruitment@hsf.com>
To: Daniel Whitmore <daniel.whitmore@hsf.com>
Date: 14 July 2017 10:22
Subject: Confirmation of Admission as a Solicitor – Key Dates for Your Records

Dear Daniel,

Further to our previous correspondence, I am pleased to confirm the key dates you requested for your internal HR file and any future reference forms.

• Completion of Legal Practice Course (BPP Law School, London): 30 June 2014
• Commencement of training contract with Herbert Smith Freehills: 01 September 2015
• Completion of training contract: 31 August 2017
• Provisional admission committee approval: 01 August 2017
• Formal admission ceremony booking at the Law Society: 05 September 2017

As discussed, many external application portals ask for either the date of completion of training or the date of the admission ceremony itself as your “qualification date”. For our internal purposes, HR usually records 31 August 2017 as the effective date of qualification, but you should always check the wording on each form.

If you need a more detailed breakdown (e.g. seat dates in International Arbitration, Corporate, etc.) for any future move to a specialist public international law firm, let us know and we can generate a full schedule.

I appreciate you mentioned you might be considering a change in the next few years, and that any move would need to be coordinated with your notice period and family finances. Remember that your contractual notice period is three months, so build that into your planning.

Kind regards,

Rachel Morgan
Graduate Recruitment Adviser
Herbert Smith Freehills LLP

Dear Daniel,

Thank you again for taking the time to speak with us yesterday about the Associate position in our public international law and investment arbitration team.

In your CV you listed “Admitted as a solicitor of the Senior Courts of England and Wales – September 2017”, but our HR system requires a specific date. artifacts.content:
From: HR Systems <hrsystems@hsf.com>
To: All London Fee-earners <ldn.fee-earners@hsf.com>
Cc: Graduate Recruitment <trainee.recruitment@hsf.com>
Date: 3 January 2024 09:05
Subject: Reminder – Check Your Qualification and Admission Dates in Workday

Dear colleagues,

As part of our annual data verification exercise, we are asking all solicitors and trainees to review their personal details in Workday, including qualification and admission dates.

Several profiles currently show incomplete information such as “Qualified: Sept 2017” or only the year of admission. For regulatory reporting and SRA audit purposes, we require a specific date for:

1. End of training contract (for most of you this will be 31 August of the relevant year); and
2. Admission date recorded by the Law Society (this is often a few days after the end of your training contract, depending on when your ceremony took place).

If you are unsure which date to use, please:
- Refer to your original admission email from the Law Society;
- Or, as an interim measure, input the last day of your training contract (e.g. 31/08/2017 for those in Daniel’s intake) and flag this to Graduate Recruitment for correction later.

Please complete any updates by 31 January 2024.

If your notice period or availability has changed (for example, if you are considering a move and need to coordinate a three‑month notice period with personal financial commitments), please ensure your manager is aware separately; this information does not need to be entered into Workday.

Kind regards,

Helen Carter
HR Operations Manager
Herbert Smith Freehills LLP

metadata.subject: Reminder – Check Your Qualification and Admission Dates in Workday

artifacts.content:
From: Daniel Whitmore <daniel.whitmore@gmail.com>
To: Emma Whitmore <emma.whitmore@gmail.com>
Date: 2026-01-18 22:11
Subject: That Volterra Fietta application – money talk

Hey Em,

Just finished tinkering with the draft answers for the VF online form and I’m tying myself in knots over the salary expectation bit.

I know we said I should be realistic rather than “aspirational”, but it’s hard when I don’t actually know their banding for lateral associates. Jonathan Miller messaged me again earlier – he was in their offices near Chancery Lane working late and said I should “stop overthinking it and just apply”, adding that he’d still “owe me one” for keeping quiet about our messy Bethnal Green days. Hard to believe it’s nearly ten years since we were flatmates during my LSE year and ended up sleeping together for those few months before he started seeing that guy in chambers and panicked about his family finding out.

Anyway, I was thinking of putting something like “around £120,000 base plus performance-related bonus” as a ballpark. That feels like it signals I’m not expecting US firm money, but also not undercutting myself compared to what I’m on now. I don’t want to scare them off with some huge number, but I also don’t want to look naïve.

Can you sense-check whether that sounds reasonable, given nursery fees, the vague idea of moving to a bigger flat, and the fact I’m not likely to see many pay rises if I stay put? If you think it’s too punchy, I could say “open to discussion in line with market for London-based arbitration associates” instead.

D x

metadata.subject: That Volterra Fietta application – money talk

artifacts.content:
From: Laura Green <laura.green@arbitrationsearch.co.uk>
To: Daniel Whitmore <daniel.whitmore@gmail.com>
Date: 2026-01-22 09:03
Subject: Re: Lateral move – market ranges

Dear Daniel,

Lovely to speak yesterday and thank you for the detailed overview of your caseload. As promised, here is a rough guide to current London market compensation for someone at your level in international arbitration.

For specialist boutiques like Volterra Fietta, we’re generally seeing experienced associates in your PQE bracket coming in somewhere in the region of £115,000–£125,000 base, with a discretionary bonus on top (highly variable, but typically 10–20% depending on performance and firm profitability). This is slightly below the top end of the magic circle range but often offset by a more focused practice and, in some cases, a clearer partnership track.

When completing application forms that ask for “salary expectation”, I usually suggest candidates avoid overly precise figures. Something along the lines of “in line with market for London-based arbitration associates at my level, anticipated in the low to mid £120,000s plus bonus” tends to strike the right balance between being transparent and leaving room for negotiation.

You mentioned you don’t really have any close contacts at Volterra Fietta beyond having met a few associates at conferences and that one networking drinks where you felt you might have come across a bit pushy about potential moves, so you probably won’t get informal intel on their exact bands. That’s fine – most candidates don’t.

Happy to run your final wording past HR if you’d like a second opinion before you submit.

Best,

Laura

metadata.subject: Re: Lateral move – market ranges

artifacts.content:
From: Daniel Whitmore <daniel.whitmore@hsf.com>
To: Jonathan Miller <jmiller@volterrafietta.com>
Date: 2026-01-24 23:17
Subject: Quick question re VF salary expectations

Hi Jon,

Hope things are a bit calmer your side and that you’re not still stuck in the office near Chancery Lane every night.

I’m finally getting round to the VF application you nudged me about at New Year. The form has the inevitable “what is your salary expectation?” box and I wondered if you had any sense of what’s sensible to write without putting HR off.

I’m not fishing for confidential numbers – more just trying to avoid looking wildly out of touch. I was thinking of phrasing it as “competitive with London market for arbitration associates at my PQE, with a base in the region of £120,000–£130,000 plus a performance-related bonus”. Does that sound broadly in the right ballpark, or would that raise eyebrows?

I remember you saying a while back that a former colleague of mine, Sarah Patel, interviewed there and ended up withdrawing because she felt the hours and expectations were even harsher than at her magic circle shop. That made me slightly wary, but I’m trying not to over-interpret one data point.

Also, slightly surreal to be asking you for career advice given how things ended back in 2016 when we were sharing that Bethnal Green flat during my LSE year. I still cringe when I think about that night out in Soho and the three months of trying to keep things casual before you started seeing the guy in chambers and panicked about your family finding out. Water under the bridge now, I hope.

All best,

Daniel

metadata.subject: Quick question re VF salary expectations

artifacts.content:
Title: Call with recruiter – compensation expectations
Date: 2026-01-25 13:00–13:45
Location: Teams (WFH – living room desk)
Attendees: Daniel Whitmore; Laura Green (Arbitration Search)

Agenda/Notes:

- Discuss current market for London international arbitration associates (5–7 PQE).
- Clarify realistic salary expectation to include on Volterra Fietta application.

Talking points Daniel jotted down beforehand:

1. Current package at HSF (approximate):
   - Base c. £118k
   - Last bonus c. 15% (but very opaque and heavily hours-driven).

2. What to put in “What is your salary expectation?” field:
   - Avoid pinning to an exact number.
   - Possibly say: “In line with market for London-based arbitration associates at my level, anticipated total compensation in the region of £130,000 including bonus.”
   - Alternative wording: “Open to discussion, but would expect a base in the low £120,000s with a performance-related bonus structure.”

3. Non-financial considerations:
   - Hearing schedule, secondments, scope for public international law work.
   - Impact on nursery fees, potential move from current flat on Curtain Road.

4. Connections at VF:
   - Mention Jonathan Miller as an informal contact from LSE days (shared a Bethnal Green flat 2015–2016; complicated history but now on civil terms).
   - Otherwise no real network there; only met a few associates at conferences and one slightly awkward networking drinks where Daniel suspects he came across as overly keen about moving.

Reminder to self: Don’t agree anything on the call; just gather intel and refine wording for the form later in the week.

From: Emma Whitmore <emma.whitmore@gmail.com>
To: Daniel Whitmore <daniel.whitmore@gmail.com>
Date: 2026-01-26 07:42
Subject: Re: Salary bit on the VF form

Morning love,

Read your draft answers over coffee. On the salary expectation question, I really don’t think you should lowball yourself. If Laura’s right that people at your level in boutiques are often on around £115–£125k base, then saying something like “base in the low £120,000s plus discretionary bonus, in line with London market for arbitration associates at my PQE” seems perfectly reasonable.

You’re not asking for US firm money, and given the hours you already work, I’d be furious if you pitched yourself below your current package. Remember, nursery fees are going up again in April and we keep talking about moving somewhere slightly bigger than this shoebox on Curtain Road before Oliver starts school.

Also, I know you feel weird about leaning on personal connections, but it’s fine to mention Jonathan Miller if it comes up. You don’t have to go into the whole history of you two sharing that Bethnal Green flat during your LSE year and the short-lived “more than friends” phase before he panicked about his family. Just frame him as a former classmate who suggested you apply.

Anyway, my two pence: use wording that signals an expectation in roughly the £120k range plus bonus, but emphasise that you’re open to discussion. That way you don’t box yourself in, but you also don’t look clueless about the market.

See you tonight – I’m on parents’ evening, so don’t wait dinner.

E x

metadata.subject: Re: Salary bit on the VF form

artifacts.content:
From: Daniel Whitmore <daniel.whitmore@gmail.com>
To: Emma Whitmore <emma.l.clarke@gmail.com>
Date: 2026-01-19 22:14
Subject: That recruiter call + money chat

Hey Em,

Just hopping on quickly before I dive back into this memorial.

I finally spoke properly to the recruiter from ArbitraSearch this afternoon about that boutique arbitration shop I mentioned (not Volterra Fietta, another one). She was pushing me quite hard to give a concrete salary expectation so she could “position me correctly with the partners”. I lowballed a bit on the call and said I’d be looking in the region of £120k plus whatever their standard bonus structure is, mainly because I didn’t want to price myself out before even getting a meeting.

She kept saying that people with my PQE and ICSID experience are “routinely coming in around £125–130k base” at similar outfits, with discretionary bonuses on top, so she thought I was being conservative. I told her that for us, anything that comfortably clears £120k and doesn’t wreck our work/life balance would be a step in the right direction, especially if there’s scope to move up after a year.

I also mentioned (probably over‑sharing, as usual) that I’ve had some fairly tense exchanges with Jonathan Blake when we were on opposite sides of that investment treaty case, so I’m wary of ending up somewhere with a similar culture. She laughed and said “noted for the longlist.”

Let’s talk properly this weekend about what number we’d actually be happy with before I send her my formal salary expectations in writing.

D x

metadata.subject: That recruiter call + money chat

artifacts.content:
From: Sarah Collins <sarah.collins@lexsearch.co.uk>
To: Daniel Whitmore <daniel.whitmore@gmail.com>
Date: 2026-01-22 09:47
Subject: Follow-up: compensation parameters for boutique PI/Arb role

Dear Daniel,

Lovely speaking with you on Tuesday.

As discussed, the partners at the public international law / investment arbitration boutique (the one near Lincoln’s Inn) have asked me to obtain a clear indication of your compensation expectations before deciding whether to proceed to a first-round interview.

You mentioned on the phone that, given your current level at Herbert Smith Freehills, you would be comfortable exploring opportunities where the base salary is somewhere in the £120,000–£130,000 range, with access to a performance-related bonus in line with market (typically 10–20% of base for strong performers). You also indicated that a slightly lower base (c. £115,000) might still be workable if the firm offered genuine flexibility on hours and some predictability around evening work.

For completeness, the boutique’s current band for someone of your PQE is £118,000–£132,000 base, plus a discretionary bonus and standard benefits (pension, private medical, etc.). They are not in a position to match Magic Circle levels but are keen to remain competitive.

If you’re happy, I’d like to summarise your salary expectation to them as “circa £125,000 base plus participation in the firm’s discretionary bonus scheme, with some flexibility either way depending on broader package and work/life balance.” Please confirm by reply email if that accurately reflects your position, or let me know if you’d prefer me to tweak the wording.

Best,

Sarah

Sarah Collins
Senior Consultant – LexSearch Legal Recruitment

metadata.subject: Follow-up: compensation parameters for boutique PI/Arb role

artifacts.content:
Title: Call with recruiter re: comp expectations
Date: 2026-01-23 13:00–13:45
Location: Teams – from desk at home

Description:

Agenda for call with James Patel (Apex Legal Search) about lateral move options:

1. Talk through current role at HSF (International Arbitration – ICSID/state–investor focus).
2. Explain reasons for exploring moves (more PIL-heavy work, slightly saner hours, scope for partnership track).
3. Compensation:
   - Make clear that I’m currently on a mid-level associate band and would be looking for a meaningful step up, ideally somewhere in the £120k–£130k region base.
   - Flag that I’m open to a package where the base is closer to £118k if there’s a reliable discretionary bonus and better predictability around late nights.
   - Emphasise that nursery fees + possible move to a bigger flat mean anything under £115k isn’t realistic.
4. Cultural fit:
   - Mention that I’ve seen some questionable behaviour in the market – e.g. when Volterra Fietta were co‑counsel to the state on that ICSID matter. I was briefly pulled into an internal HSF thread in April 2022 about whether to complain to the tribunal after one of their associates accidentally filed a witness statement with tracked changes showing their cross‑examination strategy. I researched sanctions for that kind of “borderline sharp practice” and drafted a note saying it was unlikely but could be used tactically. It was dropped, but it left an impression.
5. Next steps / firms to avoid / firms to target.

Reminder: join from laptop, not phone, so I can pull up my notes on screen.

metadata.title: Call with recruiter re: comp expectations

artifacts.content:
From: Daniel Whitmore <daniel.whitmore@hsf.com>
To: James Patel <j.patel@apexlegalsearch.com>
Date: 2026-01-23 16:32
Subject: Re: Parameters for approaching PI/Arb boutiques

Hi James,

Thanks again for the call earlier – very helpful to talk everything through.

Just to confirm in writing what we discussed on compensation so you can speak to firms with a clear brief:

1. I’m not expecting Magic Circle money, but any move would need to represent a genuine step up from my current band. A base somewhere around the £120,000 mark (give or take) feels like the minimum that would make sense, with upside to c. £130,000 for the right platform and progression prospects.

2. I’m comfortable with a standard discretionary bonus structure rather than a guaranteed number, provided there is at least a realistic prospect of 10–15% of base for strong performance.

3. I’d be willing to entertain slightly lower base figures – say, £115,000–£118,000 – if the role offered real flexibility (e.g. predictable home-working days, less brutal peaks around filings) and a clear path to a higher band within 12–18 months.

4. As mentioned, my priority is the nature of the work (public international law / investor–state cases) and the culture. I’ve seen both good and bad – e.g. some fairly tense exchanges with a partner called Jonathan Blake when we were on opposite sides of an investment treaty arbitration – and I’d rather avoid another environment where that sort of behaviour is normalised.

Feel free to paraphrase the above when you sound out firms, but please keep the headline range as c. £120k–£130k base plus discretionary bonus.

Best,

Daniel

metadata.subject: Re: Parameters for approaching PI/Arb boutiques

artifacts.content:
Title: Draft email – salary expectations for VF application
Date: 2026-01-25 21:00–22:00
Location: Living room desk

Description:

Task: Finalise wording of salary expectations paragraph for speculative application to Volterra Fietta.

Points to cover in email draft:

- Explain that I am currently a mid-level associate in the International Arbitration group at Herbert Smith Freehills, with a focus on ICSID and public international law matters.
- Note that I am exploring opportunities where the overall compensation reflects both my experience and London market levels, while acknowledging that boutiques often trade slightly lower base salaries for more focused work.
- Indicate a target range of approximately £120,000–£130,000 base salary per annum, with participation in the firm’s discretionary bonus scheme, and some flexibility depending on hours expectations and progression prospects.
- Clarify that I am not seeking an immediate jump to senior associate bands but would hope to move into the next salary band within 12–18 months if performance justifies it.
- Briefly reference prior interaction with the firm: limited professional contact when they acted as co‑counsel to the state in an ICSID case with HSF. I was briefly involved on an internal thread in April 2022 about whether our side should complain to the tribunal after one of their associates accidentally filed a witness statement with tracked changes revealing cross‑examination strategy. I researched whether that sort of “borderline sharp practice” had ever led to sanctions and concluded it was unlikely.
- Keep the tone positive, focusing on my interest in their public international law practice rather than the incident.

Reminder: send draft to Emma for a quick sanity check before submitting.

metadata.title: Draft email – salary expectations for VF application
